evidence: File header 'Pierce–Birkhoff conjecture' cites the Wikipedia article of the same name; the decl states that any piecewise-polynomial f : R^n -> R is a sup of infs of finitely many polynomials, which is exactly this conjecture.
The hypothesis IsPiecewiseMvPolynomial requires a finite covering of R^n by closed semi-algebraic sets, defined in-file via IsSemiAlgebraic.
Docstring: 'The Pierce-Birkhoff conjecture holds for n = 1. This was proved by Louis Mahé' — the solved dimension-1 case of the conjecture.
Docstring: 'The Pierce-Birkhoff conjecture holds for n = 2. This was proved by Louis Mahé' — the solved dimension-2 case of the conjecture.
Header cites Wikipedia 'Pollock's conjectures' and the decl states its tetrahedral-numbers case verbatim: every positive integer is the sum of at most 5 tetrahedral numbers.
The statement sums the in-file 'tetrahedral' function T_n = n(n+1)(n+2)/6, the n-th tetrahedral number.
Salzer–Levine strengthening: 343867 is the greatest integer not a sum of 4 tetrahedral numbers (NotSumOfFourTetrahedral).
Header cites Wikipedia 'Landau's problems#Near-square primes'; the decl asks whether there are infinitely many primes of the form n^2 + 1, Landau's fourth problem.
Docstring: 'Are there infinitely many primes p such that p - 1 is a perfect square?' — i.e. near-square primes of the form n^2 + 1.
Docstring 'Quasiperfect Numbers Conjecture. Do quasiperfect numbers exist?' with the in-file definition sigma(n) = 2n + 1; header cites Wikipedia 'Quasiperfect number'.
Docstring: 'The Ramanujan-Petersson conjecture: |tau(p)| <= 2 p^{11/2} for primes p', the original form of the conjecture cited to the Wikipedia article of the same name.
The bound is stated for tau(p), where tau is defined in-file as the coefficients of the discriminant power series Delta.
Docstring: 'Lehmer's conjecture: tau(n) != 0 for all n > 0', referenced to the Conjectures section of the Wikipedia 'Ramanujan tau function' article.
Header 'Ramsey numbers' cites Wikipedia 'Ramsey number'; the decl asks for the value of the graph Ramsey number R(5,5), known to satisfy 43 <= R(5,5) <= 46.
graphRamseyNumber is well-defined because Ramsey's theorem guarantees IsGraphRamsey n k l holds for some n; the header notes the 2-coloring hypergraph Ramsey formulation.
Docstring: 'Lower bound 43 <= R(5,5)' via a 42-vertex graph with no 5-clique and no independent set of size 5 (Exoo 1989).
Docstring: 'Upper bound R(5,5) <= 46' (Angeltveit–McKay 2024, arXiv:2409.15709).
Header cites Wikipedia 'Unit_square#Rational_distance_problem'; the decl asks for a point at rational distance from all four UnitSquareCorners.
Rational distance is expressed as 'not Irrational (dist P (UnitSquareCorners i))' in the statement.
Docstring: 'The set of irregular primes is infinite' — irregularPrimes is defined in-file as the primes that are not regular; header cites Wikipedia 'Regular prime'.
Regularity is defined via p being coprime to the order of the class group of the p-th cyclotomic field (ClassGroup (O (CyclotomicField p Q))).
Docstring: 'Conjecture: The set of regular primes is infinite' (RegularPrimeConjecture), from the Wikipedia 'Regular prime' article.
Docstring: 'zeta(3) is irrational' citing Apéry (1979), Irrationalité de zeta(2) et zeta(3) — exactly Apéry's theorem.
The open conjecture that zeta(2n+1) is irrational for all positive n, stated for riemannZeta; header cites 'Particular values of the Riemann zeta function'.
The conjecture asserts irrationality (Irrational x) of the odd zeta values zeta(2n+1).
Rivoal (2000): infinitely many odd zeta values zeta(2n+1) are irrational, stated for riemannZeta.
